problem_addressed: The difficulty of obtaining reliable labeled data for supervised machine learning in medical imaging, necessitating semi-supervised or unsupervised approaches like anomaly detection.
core_claim: Anomaly detection methods effectively address medical imaging tasks like classification and segmentation by leveraging semi-supervised and unsupervised learning, reducing dependence on costly labeled data.
key_contributions: Semi-exhaustive literature review of 120 papers on anomaly detection in medical imaging, clustered into applications (ocular, chest X-ray/mammography, CT/fMRI, brain MRI, mixed domains).
Identification of dominant research trends: brain MRI applications (53.85% of papers), prevalence of deviation-based methods using autoencoders/GANs (69.23%), and dataset-driven research bias.
Demonstration of successful applications in tumor detection (7/15 brain MRI papers), pneumonia/COVID-19 detection in chest X-rays, and retina lesion segmentation in OCT.
novelty: Synthesis of lessons learned from 120 papers to establish domain-specific trends, methodological preferences (e.g., AEs/GANs), and practical advice for anomaly detection in medical imaging, highlighting the link between data availability and research focus.
method_summary: Qualitative literature review using Google Scholar and 4 search terms, filtering 120 papers to 45 relevant research items, then clustering by imaging modality and analyzing applications, methods, and motivations.
method_components: Categorization of papers by imaging modality (Tables I-V)
Analysis of applied methods (e.g., AEs, GANs, CNNs)
Identification of research motivations (e.g., labeled data scarcity)
key_innovation: The systematic clustering of anomaly detection applications across medical imaging domains, revealing the dominance of brain MRI research and deviation-based methods, and linking research trends to dataset availability.
assumptions: Anomalies in medical images can be defined as deviations from normal/healthy samples.
Publicly available datasets drive research focus in specific imaging domains.
Semi-supervised/unsupervised methods can effectively replace supervised approaches where labels are scarce.
theoretical_grounding: None explicitly provided; the paper is a qualitative review without mathematical proofs or derivations.
datasets_used: CT scans of skeleton or spines
title: Anomaly Detection in Medical Imaging - A Mini Review
